result:
Noon in late spring does not feel like noon; the world is tilted several degrees in the other direction, a silver hush instead of the brass-band clarion sun you expect. A single crow wheels high enough to be a mote, and for a moment the sight of it dizzies me, because if the sky is a blue plate then the bird is nothing—punctured transparency—and yet it has weight in my chest.  

There is a bench under an old catalpa that lost half its heart last winter. The missing limb left a pale, scooped wound slick as the inside of a shell. Sap beads out of it like kerosene tears, and ants stream up the trunk to drink. I sit because I promised no destination today; the schedule that usually stitches minutes into a belt is abandoned on the kitchen table like a half-mended sock. The tree’s massive leaves shag the light until it drips in tessellated coins onto the sidewalk, bright enough to stagger the chipmunk who lives beneath the stone wall. It freezes mid-scamper, one paw raised, debating between fear and curiosity, then launches forward and disappears into its own private Versailles of hostas.  

Across the street, Mrs. Argueta is washing her Pontiac with a garden hose that has been knotted and mended so many times the arc of water sputters and sings in falsetto, a tiny opera of pressure. Her grandson, maybe five, stands on the bumper in superhero socks, arms stretched wide as if to shout “Tah-dah!” each time the spray misses him. Sunlight ricochets off the windshield into his face; he blinks magnificently, then laughs. The sound is pure novelty, a sound cardboard cannot reproduce, the same key as wind chimes made from sea glass.  

I realize I have been holding the same breath since arriving, lungs caliper-tight with invisible responsibilities. I exhale on purpose, slowly, the way divers are taught to release their death-grip against depth. Somewhere in the middle of it a memory surfaces unrequested: my mother folding sheets on a windy day, snapping them so hard the pale exploded into broken halos of light. I was small; the sheet was a sail, I was convinced I could climb into it and drift to another country where bread tested slightly of cardamom and rivers were named after birds. Between the second and third heartbeat the memory dents the afternoon like a thumbprint on warm wax.  

A truck growls by—landscapers, probably—the bed heaped with pruned branches that still look alive, green hands reaching upward to flag down mercy. In their wake the air smells of bruised chlorophyll and two-stroke exhaust, instant nostalgia for neighborhoods I haven’t lived in yet. I find myself wishing anonymously for the drivers: May their backs not hurt tomorrow, may one of them discover a twenty-dollar bill in the pocket of yesterday’s jeans.  

And now the crow descends, sudden as a signed confession, lands on the power line overhead. It tilts its head, fixes me with one obsidian bead that holds a thousand remembered picnics. I wonder which human face most resembles an egg to it. It caws once, dry rattle of castanets, and lifts off; the line trembles, a plucked string nobody hears. Where it had perched, a single downy feather spins, catches, spins again, refusing to surrender to gravity’s small democracy.  

Time reforms around me, briefly forgiving. I understand nothing more spectacular will happen—this is the miracle one is allowed when diligent about being alive. I nod, as though sealing an agreement, and stand. The bench leaves two warm planks pressed against the backs of my thighs, temporary tattoos of having chosen to sit still.  

Going back inside, I do not click the stopwatch of my life back on. Instead I carry the catalpa wound, the feather’s refusal, the child’s battery of laughter—all of it loose change in my pockets, ready to jingle against whatever next minute arises.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
